title: Energy efficiency of quantum computers
authors: Miquel Carrasco-Codina, Pau Escofet, Paul Hilaire, Ariane Soret, Sam Nerenberg, Victor Champain, Gerard Milburn, Klara Theophilo, Sophie H. Li, Irais Bautista, Andrés Gómez, Jose Miralles, Sergi Abadal, Carmen G. Almudéver, Eduard Alarcón, Raja Yehia
abstract: How much energy does a quantum computer consume? Are they more efficient than their classical counterparts? In this work, we make a step towards answering these questions. We define the energy efficiency of a quantum computer as the ratio of the number of algorithms it can perform during a given time over the energy consumed by the hardware during this time. We analyze the most representative physical platforms currently envisioned to be used as building blocks of quantum computers: superconducting qubits, silicon spin qubits, trapped ions, neutral atoms and photonic qubits. Including insights from experts in all these technologies and taking into account algorithm compilation constraints, we discuss the advantages and inconveniences of each platform from an energy standpoint. Beyond providing concrete values of the energy consumption of current quantum computers, we lay the foundation of a framework to benchmark the energy efficiency of any future quantum computing architecture.
summary: This paper introduces a framework to benchmark the energy efficiency of different quantum computing architectures. By analyzing the power profiles of superconducting, spin, ion, atom, and photonic platforms, it highlights the trade-offs between hardware complexity and computational throughput. The work is significant for assessing the environmental and economic sustainability of future quantum technologies.
main_problem: Quantifying the energy consumption and energy efficiency of various quantum computing platforms to determine their potential sustainability compared to classical computers.
main_result: The authors establish a standardized framework for benchmarking energy efficiency (computations per Joule) and identify that while cooling is the primary energy driver in NISQ-era devices, control electronics will likely dominate as architectures scale.
method: The study uses a hardware-aware approach, defining efficiency as the ratio of algorithms performed to energy consumed, and incorporates platform-specific compilation models (accounting for routing and gate sets).
model_or_system: The analysis covers five representative platforms: superconducting qubits, silicon spin qubits, trapped ions, neutral atoms, and photonic qubits, using power values from existing hardware components.
key_observables: Energy efficiency (computations per Joule), total power consumption (P), circuit depth (D), and number of samples (N_samples).
important_parameters: Two-qubit gate time, reset time, qubit connectivity graphs, and the ratio of two-qubit gates to total layers.
main_assumptions: The model focuses on NISQ-era power consumption, excludes manufacturing/life-cycle costs, and assumes power consumption remains relatively constant between idle and active states.
figures_summary: A power breakdown chart showing the percentage of energy used by cooling, control, and classical components; and plots of energy efficiency versus circuit depth and number of possible computations over 24 hours.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining a new energy efficiency metric, followed by a platform-by-platform analysis (solid-state, atom-based, and photonic), and concludes with a discussion on scaling, limitations, and the potential rebound effect.
why_interesting: It provides a critical resource-based perspective on the scalability of quantum hardware, which is essential for understanding the long-term viability of large-scale quantum architectures and their integration with classical control systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Effect of the transpilation step. Relative depth overhead ratio of the QFT, CDKM adder and GHZ state preparation circuits for different basis gate sets in a 100 qubit quantum computer, based on the Qiskit transpiler. The labels of the gates in the x-axis correspond to the Pauli Z gate (Z), controlled gates (CX and CZ), the Hadamard gate (H), parametric rotations (RX and RZ), the phase gate (S) and the maximally entangling gate (ECR). The lower bound (dashed line) is computed taking into account all the accessible gates in Qiskit.

Figure 2: Effect of the routing for solid-state platforms. Post-routing circuit depth of a circuit as a function of D0. The three panels correspond to different ratios of layers with two-qubit gates α2q

Figure 3: Power breakdown of a superconducting-qubit computer with ∼50 qubits, according to the components shown in Table 1. In the x-axis, the components are grouped depending of their purpose in the computer. Each group has a bar that corresponds to the total accumulated power of its components. The left y-axis shows the absolute value of power consumption of the components, and the right y-axis the relative value with respect to the overall consumption of the computer.

Figure 4: Energy efficiency (left y-axis) and number of computations (right y-axis) in 24 hours of a superconducting-qubit computer depending final circuit depth, D and number of samples, Nsamples. Panel (a) contains several series with different Nsamples values and the x-axis corresponds to D. Series in panel (b) have different D values and its x-axis corresponds to Nsamples.

Figure 4, shows the energy efficiency and number of computations of a superconducting computer executing an algorithm with given D and Nsamples for 24 hours. The relation between the energy efficiency and the number of samples is inversely proportional, according to Eq. 13. For this reason, the series shown in panel (a) are equidistant in the logarithmic scale, separated exactly by an order of magnitude. One can see that the dependency of EE with the post compilation depth D presents a different behaviour. In panel (b), the series corresponding to D = 10 and 100 are closer together than the rest.
